content.intro: When you encounter a new dataset, there is a process. Analysts do not dive in randomly — they orient themselves first.

blocks.content: Every dataset is a table. Rows are records — individual observations. Columns are fields — the attributes of each observation. Before analysing anything, you need to understand what you're looking at.
Notice that customer C-204 appears twice. In a real dataset, you would want to know: is that intentional? Are repeat customers expected? Missing context is one of an analyst's biggest challenges.
When you first open a dataset, ask yourself five questions in order:

items.label: What does each row represent?
items.detail: One order? One customer? One event?

items.label: What time period does this cover?
items.detail: A day, a quarter, all time?

items.label: Are there missing values?
items.detail: Blank cells are decisions waiting to be made.

items.label: What does each column mean?
items.detail: Never assume — read the data dictionary if one exists.

blocks.question: In the orders table above, customer C-204 appears in two rows. What does this tell you?
options: There is a data error — each customer should only appear once
The table has one row per order, and C-204 has placed two orders
The order_id column is not unique
blocks.explanation: The row represents an order, not a customer. A customer can have many orders, so seeing C-204 twice means they placed two separate orders. This is expected behaviour in an orders table.
